summary: Otto von Bismarck (1815-1898) was the "Iron Chancellor" of Prussia and the first chancellor of the unified German Empire, who pursued a foreign policy of realpolitik and famously declared that the great questions of the age would be settled by "blood and iron." He engineered German unification through the Franco-Prussian War (helped along by his editing of the Ems Dispatch), waged the anti-Catholic Kulturkampf, banned the Social Democratic Party under his Anti-Socialist Laws while simultaneously creating the first modern welfare state to undercut its appeal, and organized the European Scramble for Africa at the Berlin Conference, before being dismissed by Kaiser Wilhelm II in 1890, an event memorialized in John Tenniel's cartoon "Dropping the Pilot."

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: Bismarck is most often identified in giveaways as the "Iron Chancellor" -- a Prussian statesman who became the first chancellor of the unified German Empire and pursued a pragmatic foreign-policy philosophy known as realpolitik.

examples: Name this "Iron Chancellor" who helped unify Germany in the mid-to-late 1800s.
The Ems Dispatch was sent to this Prussian statesman, known as the architect of the German Empire and nicknamed the Iron Chancellor.

clues.clue: The first chancellor of the unified German Empire, a Prussian statesman who pursued the pragmatic foreign-policy philosophy of realpolitik
examples: For 10, name this first chancellor of a unified German Empire, who instigated the kulturkampf and pursued realpolitik.
This follower of realpolitik believed in "blood and iron."

works.name: Early Life & Rise in Prussian Politics
works.description: Before becoming chancellor, Bismarck rose through conservative Prussian political circles: while managing his father Alexander's estate, he entered the conservative religious social circle of the von Gerlach brothers and began contributing to the anti-revolutionary newspaper Kreuz-Zeitung, before King Frederick William IV selected him to represent Prussia at the Diet of Frankfurt.

clues.clue: Began contributing to the anti-revolutionary newspaper Kreuz-Zeitung after entering the conservative religious social circle of the von Gerlach brothers while managing his father Alexander's estate
examples: This leader began contributing to the anti-revolutionary newspaper Kreuz-Zeitung after entering the conservative religious social circle of the von Gerlach brothers while managing his father Alexander's estate.

clues.clue: Frederick William IV selected him to represent Prussia at the Diet of Frankfurt
examples: Frederick William IV selected this man to represent his home kingdom at the Diet of Frankfurt.

works.name: Blood and Iron & the Unification of Germany
works.description: As Prussian minister-president, Bismarck engineered the unification of Germany. In an 1862 speech, he declared that the great issues of the day would not be decided by speeches and majority resolutions but through "blood and iron" ("Blut und Eisen"), a phrase that became the watchword of his drive toward German unification.

clues.clue: In an 1862 speech, he declared that the great issues of the day would be decided not by speeches and resolutions but by "blood and iron"
examples: In a famous speech, this leader said that the great issues of the day would not be decided by speeches and resolutions, but through blood and iron.
He gave an 1862 speech concluding that "iron and blood" would solve the "great questions of the day."

clues.clue: As Prussian chancellor, he led the process of German unification
examples: The unification process was led by this Prussian chancellor, who engineered the Franco-Prussian war and claimed that the great questions of the time would be decided by "blood and iron."

Before his death, Lassalle unsuccessfully lobbied for government support of the ADAV from this leader, who later banned SPD in his Anti-Socialist Laws. This "Iron Chancellor" oversaw the unification of Germany.

works.name: The Franco-Prussian War & the Ems Dispatch
works.description: Bismarck provoked the Franco-Prussian War by editing the Ems Dispatch -- a telegram from the Prussian king reporting on a diplomatic incident -- into a version that appeared to insult the French ambassador, inflaming French public opinion into war. His victory at the Battle of Sedan led to the annexation of Alsace by the newly proclaimed German Empire.

clues.clue: His editing of the Ems telegram (Dispatch) helped provoke the outbreak of the Franco-Prussian War
examples: This leader's editing of the Ems telegram lead to the outbreak of the Franco-Prussian War.
Bismarck set off the Franco-Prussian war by secretly altering this telegram to make it look as though King Wilhelm had treated the French Ambassador rudely.

clues.clue: The Ems Dispatch, a message from the Prussian king, was sent to him before an edited version was released to incite the French public toward war
examples: The Ems Dispatch was sent to this Prussian statesman, known as the architect of the German Empire and nicknamed the Iron Chancellor.

clues.clue: His victory at the Battle of Sedan led to his empire's annexation of Alsace
examples: This leader's victory at the Battle of Sedan lead to his empire's annexation of Alsace.

works.name: Kulturkampf
works.description: Bismarck enacted the Kulturkampf, a campaign against the political and social influence of the Catholic Church in Germany, implementing measures like the Falk Laws as part of the program.

clues.clue: Enacted the Kulturkampf, an anti-Catholic campaign, while declaring political questions would be settled with iron and blood
examples: The Kulturkampf program was enacted by this German chancellor who declared that the great political questions of his time would be settled with iron and blood.
For 10, name this first chancellor of a unified German Empire, who instigated the kulturkampf and pursued realpolitik.

clues.clue: Implemented the Falk Laws as part of the Kulturkampf, policies for which Rudolf Virchow coined the term
examples: Virchow coined the term for a set of policies this man implemented that included the Falk Laws.

works.name: The Great Sausage Duel with Rudolf Virchow
works.description: In an apocryphal story, the biologist Rudolf Virchow -- dubbed the "Pope of Medicine" -- supposedly challenged Bismarck to a duel to be fought by each eating a sausage, one of them infected with deadly bacteria or parasites; Bismarck reportedly declined.

clues.clue: In an apocryphal story, the biologist Rudolf Virchow challenged him to a duel in which each would eat a sausage -- one infected with deadly bacteria/parasites -- and he declined
examples: An apocryphal story relates how Rudolf Virchow challenged this man to a duel in which Virchow would eat a clown sausage and this man an infected one.

When this German chancellor challenged biologist Rudolf Virchow to a duel, Virchow allegedly proposed that they each eat a sausage, one of which would be infected by deadly bacteria. This champion of kulturkampf declined.

works.name: Anti-Socialist Laws & the SPD
works.description: Bismarck moved to suppress the growing Social Democratic Party (SPD), refusing Ferdinand Lassalle's earlier request for state support of his General German Workers' Association (ADAV) and later banning the SPD outright through the Anti-Socialist Laws, passed after two assassination attempts against Kaiser Wilhelm I gave him a pretext to silence his political enemies. The laws lapsed as soon as Bismarck was forced from office.

clues.clue: Ferdinand Lassalle unsuccessfully lobbied him for government support of the ADAV before Lassalle's death
examples: Before his death, Lassalle unsuccessfully lobbied for government support of the ADAV from this leader, who later banned SPD in his Anti-Socialist Laws.

clues.clue: Banned the SPD through Anti-Socialist Laws passed after two assassination attempts against Wilhelm I, which lapsed as soon as he was forced from office
examples: After two assassination attempts against Wilhelm I, Bismarck outlawed this left-wing political party.

Between 1878 and 1890, the SPD's activities were restricted by Anti-Socialist Laws, proposed by this man after he took advantage of two assassination attempts against the Kaiser to silence his political enemies. Those laws lapsed immediately after this man was forced from office.

works.name: Welfare State: Social Insurance Reforms
works.description: Bismarck introduced basic health insurance, accident insurance, and old-age/social security as part of a deliberate plan to undercut the Social Democratic Party's popularity, leading historians to often credit him with creating the first modern welfare state.

clues.clue: Introduced basic health insurance, accident insurance, and social security as part of a plan to undermine the SPD's popularity, making him a historians' pick for creator of the first modern welfare state
examples: This statesman introduced basic health insurance, accident insurance, and social security as part of his plan to undermine the popularity of the Social Democratic Party.

works.name: The Berlin Conference & Colonial Policy
works.description: Bismarck convened the Berlin Conference to organize European spheres of influence during the Scramble for Africa; at the conference, Russia and Austria were among the powers that gained no territory.

clues.clue: Convened the Berlin Conference, at which Russia and Austria gained no territory, to organize European spheres of influence during the Scramble for Africa
examples: Russia and Austria were among the powers that gained no territory at the Berlin Conference, which this "Iron Chancellor" of Germany convened to organize the Scramble for Africa.
This German Chancellor sought to create European spheres of influence by hosting the Berlin Conference.

works.name: Downfall: Dismissal by Wilhelm II
works.description: Bismarck was unceremoniously dismissed from office by the young Kaiser Wilhelm II in 1890, an event immortalized in John Tenniel's Punch cartoon "Dropping the Pilot," which depicted him as a ship's captain being ordered off his own boat.

clues.clue: His dismissal from office by Kaiser Wilhelm II was depicted in John Tenniel's Punch cartoon "Dropping the Pilot," which showed him as a ship's captain being ordered off his boat
examples: This longtime Prussian and German chancellor is the subject of "Dropping the Pilot."
"Dropping the Pilot" is Tenniel's depiction of Wilhelm II's dismissal of this Chancellor of Germany. This follower of realpolitik believed in "blood and iron."

comprehensive_summary: Otto von Bismarck rose through conservative Prussian political circles, entering the social circle of the von Gerlach brothers and writing for the newspaper Kreuz-Zeitung while managing his father Alexander's estate, before King Frederick William IV selected him to represent Prussia at the Diet of Frankfurt. As Prussian chancellor, Bismarck led the drive to unify Germany, famously declaring in an 1862 speech that the great issues of the day would be decided not by speeches and resolutions but by "blood and iron." He provoked the decisive Franco-Prussian War by editing the Ems Dispatch, a telegram from the Prussian king, into a version that appeared to insult the French ambassador and inflamed French public opinion toward war; his subsequent victory at the Battle of Sedan led to the new German Empire's annexation of Alsace. As chancellor of the unified empire, Bismarck waged the Kulturkampf against the political influence of the Catholic Church, implementing measures like the Falk Laws -- a term the biologist Rudolf Virchow coined, and who, in an apocryphal legend, supposedly challenged Bismarck to a duel in which each would eat a sausage, one infected with deadly parasites, which Bismarck reportedly declined. Bismarck also moved against the rising Social Democratic Party, refusing Ferdinand Lassalle's earlier request for state support of his General German Workers' Association and later banning the SPD outright through Anti-Socialist Laws passed after two assassination attempts against Kaiser Wilhelm I gave him a pretext to silence his political enemies (the laws lapsed as soon as he left office). At the same time, he introduced basic health insurance, accident insurance, and social security as part of a deliberate plan to undercut the SPD's appeal, earning him credit from many historians as the creator of the first modern welfare state. On the international stage, he convened the Berlin Conference to organize European spheres of influence during the Scramble for Africa, at which Russia and Austria gained no new territory. Bismarck's long tenure ended in 1890, when the young Kaiser Wilhelm II unceremoniously dismissed him, an event immortalized in John Tenniel's Punch cartoon "Dropping the Pilot," which depicted the elder statesman as a ship's captain being ordered off his own boat.
